With certain exceptions, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted. I've been getting an increasing number of people trying to post after these are closed, so will have to draw a harder line than before.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says. 

The image?  That's the thirtieth card in The Sacred Geometry Oracle. Card 30, Unity of the Primary Roots, when upright tells you that you can find a reconciliation among the competing forces affecting the situation; when reversed, it warns you that what's happening will unfold in ways you can't predict, and will seem paradoxical to you. The sun in the upper left corner of the image tells you that this card belongs to the final third of the oracle, which corresponds to Nwyfre, the principle of spirit and meaning.  We've completed our passage through the first two of the basic root functions of sacred geometry -- √3, the principle of the vesica piscis and the equilateral triangle, and √2, the principle of the square and its diagonal -- and now we're working with the √5, the seed from which the Golden Section unfolds and resolves all back into unity.

[IMAGE:1]

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button above to access my online tip jar. comments.content:
I recently bought a copy of your book A Magical Education. It’s wonderful and the essays are so fresh, even at 10-20 years old—thank you so much for it.

I am wondering if you can point me to more resources on Renaissance magic. Are there books or authors you recommend for studying it? I have a background of almost 20 years studying astrology, including mundane and horary. I’m very interested in the Neoplatonists as well.

I cannot practice ceremonial magic at the moment as I have young children, but I’m also curious for future reference which of your series of books/systems would be most pertinent to an interest in Renaissance magic (and astrological magic).

Many thanks!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Sure. If you're ready to jump in the deep end, Cornelius Agrippa's Three Books of Occult Philosophy is the classic way in. If you want to take it a little more gradually, Chris Warnock has some excellent books on Renaissance astrological magic, available via his site renaissanceastrology.com . I've written almost nothing about it -- The Art and Practice of Geomancy covers a few things in presenting geomancy from a Renaissance point of view, but there's so much good material in primary sources from the period itself I'd encourage you to go for that.

comments.content:
Dear Archdruid Emeritus,

Blessings to you and my fellow commentators!

1. How important is having an applying aspect in an election? Last week, you wrote, “If you have Saturn ruling the 7th, make sure that Saturn isn't debilitated in your election, and if you can have Venus applying to a neutral or well dignified Saturn by trine or sextile in your working, the results will be even better.” I found such a date on May 18, 2023, when Venus (in Cancer) will have a separating trine with Saturn (in Pisces). In addition, the Moon (in Taurus) will have a separating sextile with Venus. It’s an almost perfect election if not for the separating aspects.

2. Is there any connection between personal success and the 8th House? In traditional astrology, it is seen as an unfortunate house, connected with death, fear and anxiety. Yet, I have read and seen, for example, that many wealthy individuals have planets in the 8th, sometimes even stelliums.

Many thanks,
Jean Luc

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Separating aspects are traditionally ineffective for most elections, since they relate to opportunities that have already passed by. If something good has happened and you want to hang onto it, a separating election can work, and of course malefic elections can make use of them, but the less said about those the better.

2) The 8th can be very prosperous indeed. First of all, it's the 2nd from the 7th -- the money or property of the partner. If you have a well-dignified planet in the 8th, or the ruler of the 8th well dignified, you can expect to benefit financially from marriage or partnerships. The 8th is also the house of inheritance, and a well-dignified 8th can bring you plenty of money in someone's will.

Dear JMG,

where can I find the names of God corresponding to the 22 paths on the tree of life?

For example, the 32nd path is ruled by Saturn, so the name is YHVH Elohim. And I assume it goes for other paths ruled by planets - just use the name that corresponds to the planet. Elemental Fire, water and air names I know from the ritual of the pentagram.

But some paths correspond to zodiac signs, and I don't know what names to use for them.

But I'm sure somebody already has this information in a table.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: For the zodiacal paths, use the element that rules the sign. There are tables -- you can find them in Regardie's The Golden Dawn among other places -- but the rule is simple enough: elemental paths have the elemental divine names, planetary paths have the planetary divine names, zodiacal paths have the elemental divine names again. Note that you can also use ADNI, the name for Earth, on the 32nd Path, and AHIH, the name for Spirit, on the 31st.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thank you for your prompt reply.

replies.title: the paths again
replies.content:
I do have another question about the paths, JMG.

The existing correspondences between the paths on the tree of life and the Hebrew letters work.

But wouldn't it be more logical to assign the three mother letters to the three horizontal paths, the seven double letters to the seven vertical paths, and the twelve single letters to the twelve diagonal paths?

It just fits so naturally. Did anyone ever write about that? It's just that many occult writers go to great lengths to make you notice that there are three horizontal, seven vertical, and twelve diagonal paths.

replies.title: Re: the paths again

Last night, though, something new: a faint, delicate bell. Followed by a bark from a small dog. We don’t have a dog; the neighbor dogs are huge, and are indoors at that hour. Husband slept through it all.

Would these be random astral sounds? If so, anything I ought to do, other than just roll over and go back to sleep? If someone wanted my attention surely there are better ways of communicating that?

comments.title: The Magical Battle of Britain
comments.content:
In her war letter #87 Dion Fortune writes:

"[...] We too can evoke primordial energies from the primitive levels of the national group soul [...] When the Germans open up the primordial levels of their racial mind they release the elemental energies of the old gods - the bloodstained, mindless images of the heros of Norse myth. [...] The Kelts of these islands were among the earliest to be Christianised, as the Germans were among the latest [...] not the mindless heroes of Valhalla, but the chivalry of the Table Round; Excalibur instead of Nothung, and the Quest of the Grail instead of the looting of the Rhinegold. [...]"

I realize that one can write a probably very fascinating book based on this statement when the underlying history, myths and magic are properly researched. Unfortunately I have to push this at the very far end of a very long to-do-list, so it's going to take a while...

So for a start maybe just a few "small" questions:

1) I wonder if I'm grossly distorting something if I sum up what she says here with "My spirituality is better than yours (because I got it first)." From what I knew before reading it myself I got the impression that it was for a big part "raspberry jam" that made Fortunes strategy a success. And based on this I wonder if the raspberry jam she generated with the impression of superiority she promoted has something to do with the history of Britain that unfolded after WW II. At least, based on my limited understanding I sense that there could be a flaw in her strategy that didn't matter (or was even necessary) for securing victory but may have spoiled it's fruits?

2a) Disregarding a potential strategic flaw, she touches on the same theme as did Jung when he wrote "Wotan" and I don't think it can be denied that if you "toy around" with the imagery of Götterdämmerung, you probably get one. And one can also say that (as you once put it, if I remember correctly) [Germanic] myths always end with one sort of Götterdämmerung or another. But these myths are "written history". There is, however, mythology that's not yet entirely written and I wonder if history had taken a different road if the magical warfare of the Germans had been based on the imagery of the surviving Gods, like Vidar, for example. But then I guess they (the German leadership at that time) would have had to be different personalities altogether, which might prove Fortunes point. To what extend is this voluntary act of the German magicians or a build-in flaw of spirituality based on Norse gods?

2b) This leads me to the question that bothers me probably most: Concerning the very dominant role "Götterdämmerung" plays in Heathen mythology - If you want to follow a (long-lasting, healthy) spiritual path that might have the chance to take you beyond incarnation on the material plane based on Heathenry - is it at some point necessary to "invent history" and "write" the chapters that come after Ragnarök? If myths are stories that never happened but always are - it should be possible to find them somewhere, shouldn't it? But how does this happen (if only on an individual level)?

Ok thanks for your patience so far - I guess I am asking a lot...

Greetings,
Nachtgurke

replies.title: Re: The Magical Battle of Britain
replies.content:
1) It's a little more nuanced than that, but not much. Keep in mind that Fortune had the prejudices of her place and time, and a very hard prejudice against Germany and Germans was an important part of that. As for the raspberry jam factor, Britain had a much easier time letting go of its empire than most nations do, so it doesn't seem to have amounted to much.

2a) It was a flaw on the part of the Nazi movement. The German cultural resurgence out of which they emerged included elements of both sides of the Germanic mythos, pre- and post-Götterdämmerung; there was a very Vidar-esque element to the Wandervogel movement and that whole end of German youth culture, which you can see with great clarity in Hesse's novel Demian. Hitler was fixated on the pre-Götterdämmerung side of things, however --that's why he banned performances of Parsifal, Wagner's post-Götterdämmerung opera, during the war years -- and so got what he asked for.

2b) Instead of writing those chapters, you'd want to invoke the gods of that age -- Vidar and Vali, Modi and Magni, Hoenir, et al. -- and ask them to prophesy.

comments.title: Solar and Telluric currents
comments.content:
Last week I was reading the Druid Magic Handbook. I am at the chapter on the Solar and Telluric currents. Later on I was listening to a Jerm Warfare podcast about Polar Bears. It stuck me that all people talk about when they discuss the temperature is the warming power of the sun.

But what about the Earth itself? The inner part of our planet consists of seas of solid metals. The core of our planet is solid and even warmer. replies.title: Re: Solar and Telluric currents
replies.content: The internal heat of the earth is a significant factor in deep mines. If I understand correctly, it's a combination of heat left over from the origins of the planet -- it was white-hot when it formed -- and heat released by the decay of radioactive isotopes. Since there's so much rock in the way, it doesn't contribute much heat to the surface, but you're right that it's a factor that has been neglected.

replies.title: Re: Solar and Telluric currents
replies.content:
Thanks. I've read some more and it's like you say. It looks like the earth is separated into a crust, a mantle, a molten core, and a solid core. There are three sources of heat:

  • Leftover heat from the formation of the Earth
  • Liquid metals in the molten core solidify (this process creates heat)
  • Most (90%) of the heat is due to radioactive decay in the mantle

The fact that the mantle is the heat source means the inner core is cooler than the outer core. The inner core is solid metal. The solid inner core expands at a rate of 1cm per 1000 years as the Earth cools down.

The outer core is an ocean of molten metal. The Earth's rotation makes this ocean flow and swirl, and the moving metal generates the planet's magnetic field. The magnetic field prevents the solar wind from blowing away the atmosphere.

The Earth contributes 47 TW heat flow to the surface, which is dwarfed by the Sun's 173.000 TW. So it looks like the Telluric heat flow is neglected for a good reason :)

comments.title: Historical question
comments.content:
Good Morning, JMG:
Have been meditating on threads from the last 2 Magic Mondays regarding possible awakening of Germanic gods.
Is there anything in occult lore about the relationship/connection/etc. between ancient Germanic gods and ancient Celtic gods, and the respective people?
One notes that ancient Germanic people seem to have had sacred groves before the Romans (Appolonian culture) destroyed them, which sounds very Druidy; barrows and stone circles are found in what is now Britain, France, and Germany; your essay on the myth of Einigan discussed the ancient figure of Mannas, which seems to be common to both pre-Germanic and pre-Celtic; layperson reading of current archaeology seems to indicate La Tene culture, Bell Beaker, and Grooved Ware are all related to each other. My curiosity is piqued about this because, as you have pointed out, the river valleys of the Thames and the Rhine are where Faustian culture was born after the pseudomorphoses of Appolonian and Magian influence.
I get the sense that the pre-Appolonian landscape of Europe was something akin to pre-Columbian North America, with a dynamic mix of tribes in different parts of the land, with an equally dynamic mix of spiritual experiences, some of them shared across tribes and regions, some of them not. This seems relevant given the differing ways that Faustian history has played out when one compares the British Isles and Germany.
I doubt I am the first one to whom this thought has occurred, so am wondering what may already be out there in the occult lore...

replies.title: Re: Historical question
replies.content: There certainly was a very porous boundary between 'Celtic' and 'Germanic' peoples in ancient northern Europe. The Belgae and Cimbri spring to mind as possible examples of Celto-Germanic tribes. And then there's that rather fascinating La Tene cauldron found at Gundestrup in Denmark...

replies.title: Re: Historical question
replies.content: There were very extensive interchanges between Celtic and Germanic peoples before the collapse of Rome set everyone at everyone else's throats. Michael Enright's book Lady with a Mead Cup explores these connections. I haven't seen much on the subject in the occult literature, though at least one Heathen who works with Anglo-Saxon traditions recognizes that druidry was a known practice in Old English culture -- Alfred the Great passed laws forbidding his subjects to practice it!

replies.title: Re: Historical question
replies.content:
Our host beat me to recommending Lady with a Mead Cup. A few other thoughts that might give you further rabbit holes to go down:

1) Some Indo-European historical linguists posit a "Proto-Celto-Germanic" branch that was the common ancestor of both the Celtic and Germanic languages, but others believe that speakers of proto-Celtic and proto-Germanic each split off directly from proto-Indo-European. This is further complicated by the next point. If you'd like to look at the backstory for this in a lot of detail, The Horse, the Wheel, and Language by David Anthony is a wonderful multi-disciplinary survey of what we know about the people who spoke Proto-Indo-European, and it goes up until each distinct daughter language split off.

2) From my reading, it seems like for a certain time period, the Celts were a prestige culture to the early Germans (I'm thinking here of La Tene culture and how you start seeing stuff from La Tene culture in elite burials farther north). If you're unfamiliar with the terminology, "prestige culture" is a culture that other cultures attempt to imitate because they are seen as fancier or better in some way. Think of how some modern Americans affect Europeanisms or how folks around the world affect Americanisms. So, that makes it really hard to sort out which archaeological findings are Celts, which Germans, which are Celt-influenced Germans, and whether these labels are even meaningful! Likewise for linguistic evidence - if Proto-Germanic speakers were adopting vocabulary and/or sounds from speakers of Proto-Celtic, that would be very hard to distinguish from both groups sharing a distinct linguistic ancestor more recently than Proto-Indo-European.

3) Going rather a lot later, when we start having some actual historical records, rather than just archaeology, historical linguistics, and ancient DNA, we run into some further sources of confusion. I've seen it argued that Romans like Caesar basically called anyone east of the Rhine a "German" and anyone west a "Celt", but that it was likely a much more complex picture than that (for example, you see Casesar give the name for a "Germanic" tribe that linguistically looks Celtic, or vice versa).

4) One rabbit hole I have not yet gone down are the books by Jean Manco: Blood of the Celts, Origins of the Anglo-Saxons, and Ancestral Journeys. They're surveys of the ancient DNA evidence, which is a topic I've read about quite a bit at the blog West Hunter by Greg Cochran (and formerly also Henry Harpending), the co-authors of The 10,000 Year Explosion. Obviously DNA does not equal culture, but it's another piece of the puzzle.

Hope these help!
Jeff

comments.content:
What's the deal with C.S. Lewis and the occult? Clearly he knew the medieval and Renaissance world view, inside and out, and both the space trilogy and his Narnia books are rich with hidden and not-so-hidden esoteric and occult meaning, as well as pagan gods/goddesses/demigods/themes. I know he was friends with Owen Barfield. Was Lewis an occultist? I haven't been able to find a clear answer via Google.

Related question (and I would love to hear theories on this from everyone), how the heck have the Narnia books become such mainstays of evangelical Christian Protestant reading lists and education when they are *full* of magic and the occult? Just one example: in the Voyage of the Dawn Treader, the main characters are helped by a literal magician named Coriakin who practices spells and conjuring, and who assists Lucy, the spiritual heart of the whole series, with a spell. Bacchus cavorts around in The Lion, the Witch, and the Wardrobe, which also features an awakened River God (all positively depicted).

From what I can tell, there are a few Evangelical grumbles here and there about the books but they are very much on the fringe. Is there some kind of enchantment on these books that keeps the Evangelicals buying, reading, and building their children's curriculums around them? (I have found them on many Christian homeschool lists and Evangelical school reading lists.) I say this in jest but actually I really wonder.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
If I may, Lewis was extremely attracted and both repulsed by the occult, a friend not only to Barfield but also to Charles Williams who I seem to remember was (at a time) a member of the Golden Dawn.
In his autobiography, Surprised by Joy, he talks a bit about this:

"She [the Matron at his school] was (as I should now put it) floundering in the mazes of Theosophy, Rosicrucianism, Spiritualism; the whole Anglo-American Occultist tradition. Nothing was further from her intention than to destroy my faith; she could not tell that the room into which she brought this candle was full of gunpowder. I had never heard of such things before; never, except in a nightmare or a fairy tale, conceived of spirits other than God and men. I had loved to read of strange sights and other worlds and unknown modes of being, but never with the slightest belief; even the phantom dwarf had only flashed on my mind for a moment. It is a great mistake to suppose that children believe the things they imagine; and I, long familiar with the whole imaginary world of Animal-Land and India (which I could not possibly believe in since I knew I was one of its creators) was as little likely as any child to make that mistake. But now, for the first time, there burst upon me the idea that there might be real marvels all about us, that the visible world might be only a curtain to conceal huge realms uncharted by my very simple theology. And that started in me something with which, on and off, I have had plenty of trouble since--the desire for the preternatural, simply as such, the passion for the Occult. Not everyone has this disease; those who have will know what I mean. I once tried to describe it in a novel. It is a spiritual lust; and like the lust of the body it has the fatal power of making everything else in the world seem uninteresting while it lasts. It is probably this passion, more even than the desire for power, which makes magicians. But the result of Miss C.'s conversation did not stop there. Little by little, unconsciously, unintentionally, she loosened the whole framework, blunted all the sharp edges, of my belief. The vagueness, the merely speculative character, of all this Occultism began to spread--yes, and to spread deliciously--to the stern truths of the creed. The whole thing became a matter of speculation: I was soon (in the famous words) "altering 'I believe' to 'one does feel'". And oh, the relief of it! Those moonlit nights in the dormitory at Belsen faded far away. From the tyrannous noon of revelation I passed into the cool evening twilight of Higher Thought, where there was nothing to be obeyed, and nothing to be believed except what was either comforting or exciting. I do not mean that Miss C. did this; better say that the Enemy did this in me, taking occasion from things she innocently said."

And later on:

"The idea that if there were Occult knowledge it was known to very few and scorned by the many became an added attraction; "we few", you will remember, was an evocative expression for me. That the means should be Magic--the most exquisitely unorthodox thing in the world, unorthodox both by Christian and by Rationalist standards--of course appealed to the rebel in me. I was already acquainted with the more depraved side of Romanticism; had read Anactoria, and Wilde, and pored upon Beardsley, not hitherto attracted, but making no moral judgement. Now I thought I began to see the point of it. In a word, you have already had in this story the World and the Flesh; now came the Devil. If there had been in the neighbourhood some elder person who dabbled in dirt of the Magical kind (such have a good nose for potential disciples) I might now be a Satanist or a maniac.
In actual fact I was wonderfully protected, and this spiritual debauch had in the end one rather good result. I was protected, first, by ignorance and incapacity. Whether Magic were possible or not, I at any rate had no teacher to start me on the path. I was protected also by cowardice; the reawakened terrors of childhood might add a spice to my greed and curiosity as long as it was daylight. Alone, and in darkness, I used my best endeavours to become a strict Materialist again; not always with success. A "Perhaps" is quite enough for the nerves to work upon. But my best protection was the known nature of Joy. This ravenous desire to break the bounds, to tear the curtain, to be in the secret, revealed itself, more and more clearly the longer I indulged it, to be quite different from the longing that is Joy. Its coarse strength betrayed it. Slowly, and with many relapses, I came to see that the magical conclusion was just as irrelevant to Joy as the erotic conclusion had been. Once again one had changed scents. If circles and pentangles and the Tetragrammaton had been tried and had in fact raised, or seemed to raise, a spirit, that might have been--if a man's nerves could stand it--extremely interesting; but the real Desirable would have evaded one, the real Desire would have been left saying, "What is this to me?"
(...)
"I had learned a wholesome antipathy to everything occult and magical which was to stand me in good stead when, at Oxford, I came to meet Magicians, Spiritualists, and the like"

I think he also said he went to see Yeats and came out quite unimpressed by the magician (In fact, the Magician in The Magician's Nephew is supposed to be based on Yeats.

Manuel

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) That's a good question. Lewis clearly knew much, much more about occultism than he was willing to admit in public; That Hideous Strength in particular is full of occult references and teachings, and I find it impossible to believe that the scene in which the planetary intelligences are summoned could have been written by anyone who'd never been present at such an evocation -- it's quite literally the best fictional description of a major magical working in print. He ran with Barfield, of course, and he also ran with Charles Williams, who served two terms as Hierophant of Waite's Golden Dawn temple and was up to his eyeballs in occultism. But I suspect he had more personal experience than any of his biographers (including himself) ever wanted to admit. One thing to keep in mind is that he was compulsively secretive -- his friends all referred to his heavily fictionalized autobiography Surprised by Joy as "Suppressed by Jack" -- Jack being the nickname he went by; if your name was Clive Staples Lewis you'd probably do the same -- because he left out so much. So he may have left out some serious occult study...

2) I think they really, truly, desperately want magic. I doubt it'll happen in my lifetime, but the return of the repressed is inevitable enough that sometime in the next century or two, I expect some form of Christian occultism to become wildly popular all through American Protestant Christianity. Until that happens, Narnia is a convenient way to skirt the edges of the craving.

This question is “out there” but since you are a “far out dude” I figure it’s worth a shot. Hopefully you won't find it too frivoulous.

It started with the ‘crop circles’: large, complex, patterns, often mandala-like and often applying sacred geometry principles and proportions that can be reproduced using a pencil, ruler and compass. This has been going on for a good three decades now.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The artists are indeed leaving some great tracks in space -- and that's exactly it.

When Doug Bower and Dave Chortley faked the first crop circles in Britain in 1976, they had no clue what they were setting in motion, and for what it's worth I think they may have been moved to do so by forces utterly beyond their imagining. It started out as a prank and then became a moneymaking scheme -- farmers found they could make a whale of a lot of money by having a crop circle show up in a field, and then charging tourists a pound or two a head for admission. (When I was in Glastonbury in 2003, you could buy postcards headed ORDERING NEXT SEASON'S CROP CIRCLES, with a typical Somerset farmer sitting by a satellite dish with headphones on, gazing expectantly at the sky, and a stack of signs saying "This Way To The Crop Circle" behind him. It was pretty blatant!)

But then the different teams of people who did crop circles started competing with one another, and amazing things started to appear. People found that the experience of helping to construct a crop circle was a ritual, a performance, the high point of the year. The circles soared in complexity and beauty -- if the people I've talked to were telling the truth, some of them were planned out in disused warehouses in the Bristol area, and the construction process rehearsed several times there, because the sheer complexity demanded that much preparation. Even after Doug and Dave did their thing and the fad crested and waned, the joy of making beautiful agroglyphs was enough to keep the process going -- and as you've pointed out, people are doing the same thing on beaches and on snow, and in other locations.

As for what effect they will have, it's too early to say, but something vivid and powerful is making its influence felt through those shapes, expressing itself through the minds and spirits of the people who are creating them. If the old sacred geometers are right, and I believe they are, the result can only be good.

comments.title: Pledge of Allegiance
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I wonder if you might say a few words about the common American ritual of reciting the pledge of allegiance. I balked at reciting it sometime in high school, when I started becoming aware of the less-than-savory actions committed by the US, in the name of its citizens, and when I wasn't sure I wanted to "pledge my allegiance" to its symbols. I'm pretty much still of that frame of mind because of the many ways it seems such a pledge could come back to bite a person (karmically at least) and the way the flag-as-a-symbol is manipulated/claimed.

I've just applied to a Grange lodge (Yay! -- though it's 20 miles away from my home, I think it's an acceptable use of whatever portion of remaining petroleum I'm allotted, especially if it helps me be more constructive in the way we land into the post-petroleum era :) )... anyway.... and this particular lodge (if not all of them) recites the pledge in their meetings.

I'm trying to figure out how I want to approach this (bring it up? bow out of the recitation -if that's even possible-? mumble incoherently through it? say the few words I agree with?), but I'm not sure what it even *is* - is it a promise, or just a ritual performance aiding in group coherence, or...?

I'd be most interested to hear your take on this.

-- tangentially, but I find it interesting that there'd have been a time a (pretty-much Christian) prayer in a nominally secular setting would've ground my gears too, but I'm happy to say that I was rather thrilled to take part in a meeting where prayer was publicly performed (and it was a prayer I could get behind, so that didn't hurt either). This change in attitude is pretty much a result of my hanging out in these parts - so, many thanks to you and your diverse commentariat.

replies.title: Re: Pledge of Allegiance
replies.content:
I learned to say the Pledge in elementary school in the 1940s, well before the words "under God" were added to it during the Eisenhower years. I've never said it with those added words at anytime in my life, and no one has ever noticed or commented on it.

People mostly hear what they expect to hear, not what you actually say. If you quietly modify the Pledge in some way or other to make it more patalable to you, and don't say it too loudly or emphatically, hardly anyone will ever notice what you've done, or even much care if they do notice.

replies.title: Re: Pledge of Allegiance
replies.content:
All Granges and most other fraternal orders do this as a matter of course. It was a protective measure put in place to chase out entryists from both sides of the political spectrum -- in the mid-20th century neither Communists nor Fascists would repeat the pledge, and so it allowed the lodges to screen out people who were trying to hijack them for political advantage. Granges and most other groups are required by the organization's laws to begin with the Pledge, so it's not negotiable.

Myself, I have no problem with the Pledge. What is this republic which has liberty and justice for all? No country on the material plane, to be sure. The republic they're invoking in that pledge is the ideal archetype of the United States, the vision -- never achieved, never possible to achieve, but always present -- of a nation whose government really is of, by, and for the people, and which does in fact provide liberty and justice for all. I recite the Pledge as an invocation of the ideal, with the intention of trying to bring the country in which I live just a little closer to that visionary goal.

replies.title: Re: Pledge of Allegiance
replies.content:
I don't know why I never noticed this before, but that's basically right there in the text itself.

"I pledge allegiance to the flag of the United States of America" - not to America itself, but to the flag. A symbol.

"...and to the republic for which it stands" - i.e. the republic symbolized by the flag. What is that republic? An indivisible (and therefore, by extension, undivided) nation, under the protection of a deity (implied by most of its speakers to be the Christian God, but that's never actually specified), with liberty and justice for all.

Presumably the republic in question exists on the astral plane (as a dream or ideal) and/or the mental (as a theoretical possibility), and maybe even on the etheric as an (admittedly, possibly polluted) egregor (e.g. the Spirit of '76). Just...not on the physical.

replies.title: Re: Pledge of Allegiance
replies.content: Exactly. Your allegiance is not to the grubby secondhand reality but to the symbol and the republic -- the res publica, the public concerns -- that it represents. That involves participation in the grubby secondhand reality, to be sure, but said reality isn't the focus of the thing.

comments.title: Meditation on the subtle senses
comments.content:
Hullo JMG & the commentariat!

I hope you are all well.

I have observations and questions mixed into this next bit of my post that I am hoping others will find useful, and maybe we could compare notes and thoughts.

Ever since I started to notice and take attention to my intuition/gut and my astral senses/imagination, I've been very curious in how to tease apart what's "really there" vs what might be self created or misunderstood. And how to contextualize the experience of "astral static" as JMG calls it.

In my experience, my intuition and astral senses don't seem to be "always right" in the sense that my ideas about what my intuitions mean are not always dead on. And or I seem to sometimes manipulate and/or misunderstand them.

As I've pondered why this is I've come up with a line of reasoning that may explain part of why this is.

I suppose that when I was a baby, my physical senses were still developing in the way that my astral senses are very much developing now. Things I saw with my eyes were probably somewhat of a confusing blur, and slowly as I used this brand new sense I began to be able to differentiate colors, and light, and objects and motion. I think it's likely all of my physical senses went through a long fine tuning period like this, before they got more or less "locked in" as I got older.

So I wonder if something very similar is happening with my Astral and other subtle senses. I think my senses are still developing.

What do you and the commentariat think of this supposition, JMG? any thoughts or experiences to share?

-WindMan

replies.title: Re: Meditation on the subtle senses
replies.content: Good. Very good. That's exactly what's going on; you're learning to figurate the astral world -- to assemble astral perceptions into a world -- in exactly the same way you learned to figurate the material world in infancy.

comments.title: Alchemy
comments.content:
Hey jmg

Would it be correct to define practical alchemy as “occult chemistry “, the study of chemical reactions caused by occult forces, just like thermochemistry is the study of chemical reactions caused by heat?

replies.title: Re: Alchemy
replies.content:
No, for two reasons.

The first is that alchemy is much, much broader than that. There are chemical alchemies, yes, but there are also biological alchemies, psychological alchemies, spiritual alchemies, medical alchemies, sexual alchemies, and the list goes on. Alchemy is not a single field of study like chemistry; it's a general method for studying anything, like science. (Thus there's an alchemical method, just as there's a scientific method.)

The second is that it's far from clear that the reactions that make alchemy possible are chemical in nature. If in fact alchemy is capable of transmuting metals -- and this is of course the central claim of one branch of the tradition -- it's clear that something beyond chemistry is at work, since chemical forces can't change the atomic number of atoms! Chemical alchemy might better be described as metachemistry, because it uses chemical methods but goes beyond the chemical realm.

comments.title: Follow up
comments.content: Perhaps another way to ask question...is magic always constrained by laws of supply and demand and scarce resources? Or is there wiggle room to make sure you're not taking at all from what would otherwise be waste or given in charity to the supplicant? Polka dot Melancholic Mole

replies.title: Re: Follow up
replies.content: It depends on what kind of magic you're doing, of course. Everything on the material plane is constrained by supply and demand, by limits, and by the other laws of physics and economics; that's why magic meant to get something for nothing so reliably blows up in people's faces. On the other hand, magic aimed at self-improvement and spiritual development is not constrained by these laws; for example, there's no limit to how lovable you can become.
